clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy is brought by his mother with a 3-week history of a painful, boggy, purulent swelling on his scalp. He was initially treated by a pharmacist with a topical antifungal cream for two weeks without improvement. On examination there is a 5 cm tender, fluctuant, inflammatory mass on the vertex with multiple pustules discharging pus through the hair follicles. Several cervical lymph nodes are palpable. Wood lamp examination is negative. What is the most important long-term risk if this condition is left untreated or inadequately treated?
options: Progression to meningitis via direct extension
Permanent scarring alopecia at the affected site
Development of systemic tinea corporis
Bacterial superinfection leading to bacteraemia
Conversion to tinea barbae in adolescence
explanation: This is a kerion — the severe inflammatory form of tinea capitis caused most often by Trichophyton tonsurans or Microsporum canis. The boggy, purulent mass with follicular pustules and lymphadenopathy is classic. Kerion represents a hypersensitivity reaction to the dermatophyte. If not promptly treated with systemic antifungals (griseofulvin or terbinafine), the intense follicular inflammation destroys hair follicles permanently, resulting in irreversible scarring alopecia in the affected area. Topical antifungals cannot penetrate the hair shaft and are ineffective for tinea capitis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Meningitis from direct extension is extremely rare and not a recognised complication of kerion; the infection is limited to the scalp hair follicles.
This is correct — destruction of hair follicles by the inflammatory process leads to permanent scarring alopecia if treatment is delayed.
Tinea corporis is a separate superficial infection; kerion does not progress systemically in immunocompetent children.
While secondary bacterial colonisation can occur in a kerion, true bacteraemia is uncommon and not the principal long-term concern.
Tinea barbae is a beard-area infection in adults and is not a progression of untreated childhood kerion.
clinicalPearl: Kerion requires systemic antifungal therapy (not topical); delay leads to permanent scarring alopecia — the most important preventable long-term complication.
keyClue: Boggy, purulent scalp mass with follicular pustules in a child after failed topical antifungal treatment
commonExamTrap: Treating kerion as a bacterial abscess and incising it, or continuing topical antifungals — both worsen outcome and risk scarring.
missedConcept: Kerion is an inflammatory tinea capitis requiring systemic antifungals; topical therapy is ineffective and the chief risk is permanent alopecia, not systemic spread.

subtopic: Ocular involvement and malignant melanoma risk
clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman with a 5-year history of stable vitiligo affecting both hands and perioral region attends for a routine follow-up. She has no other medical conditions. On examination she has well-demarcated, depigmented patches with no signs of repigmentation. She asks about her long-term prognosis and what complications she should watch for. Which of the following is the most accurate statement regarding her condition's prognosis and associated risks?
options: Her vitiligo patches carry a significantly elevated risk of malignant melanoma
The perioral distribution predicts a poor response to any treatment modality
She has an increased risk of other autoimmune conditions including thyroid disease
Vitiligo never progresses after the age of 30, so no further spread is expected
Sun exposure is beneficial for vitiligo patches as it stimulates melanocyte repopulation
explanation: Vitiligo is an autoimmune condition in which T-cell-mediated destruction of melanocytes occurs. Patients with vitiligo have a significantly higher prevalence of other organ-specific autoimmune diseases, particularly autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto thyroiditis and Graves disease), type 1 diabetes mellitus, Addison disease, and pernicious anaemia. Annual thyroid function tests are recommended. Vitiligo itself does not predispose to melanoma; in fact the depigmented patches may have a theoretical reduced melanoma risk due to absence of melanocytes.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Vitiligo patches lack melanocytes and are not associated with increased melanoma risk; melanoma arises from malignant transformation of melanocytes which are absent in vitiligo lesions.
Perioral involvement does not specifically predict poor treatment response; acrofacial vitiligo can sometimes show better repigmentation with UV therapy than acral areas.
This is correct — vitiligo is associated with a cluster of autoimmune conditions, most importantly thyroid disease, warranting periodic screening.
Vitiligo can progress at any age; new patches may appear throughout life, particularly after physical or emotional stress.
Unprotected sun exposure risks sunburn in depigmented patches (which lack photoprotective melanin) and increases skin cancer risk in those areas.
clinicalPearl: Screen all vitiligo patients annually for autoimmune thyroid disease (TFTs + anti-TPO antibodies) — the most common associated autoimmune condition.
keyClue: Young woman with vitiligo asking about long-term complications and associated risks
commonExamTrap: Assuming vitiligo patches have elevated melanoma risk — the opposite may be true since melanocytes are absent.
missedConcept: Vitiligo is a marker for autoimmune diathesis; the key follow-up concern is screening for associated autoimmune diseases, not malignancy.

clinicalStem: A 68-year-old man with a known history of psoriasis presents to the emergency department with a 48-hour history of generalised redness and scaling covering more than 90% of his body surface area. He abruptly stopped his methotrexate three weeks ago. On examination he is flushed, shivering, and confused. Temperature is 35.8°C, pulse 118 bpm, BP 88/54 mmHg, and respiratory rate 24/min. His skin is universally erythematous with fine generalised scaling. What is the most immediately life-threatening complication driving his haemodynamic instability?
options: Septicaemia from secondary bacterial skin infection
Massive cutaneous fluid and protein loss causing hypovolaemia
Cardiac failure precipitated by high-output state from skin vasodilation
Hypothermia from loss of thermoregulatory skin barrier
Acute hepatic failure from prior methotrexate toxicity
explanation: Erythroderma (exfoliative dermatitis) involves near-total skin inflammation causing a massive increase in transepidermal water loss (up to 3 L/day), protein exudation via the exfoliating scale, and disruption of the skin barrier. This leads to hypovolaemia, hypoalbuminaemia, and electrolyte disturbances, which are the primary drivers of haemodynamic instability in the acute phase. The combination of tachycardia, hypotension, and confusion in this patient reflects haemodynamic collapse from fluid and protein depletion. Immediate IV fluid resuscitation is critical.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Secondary bacterial infection is a recognised complication of erythroderma but is not the immediate explanation for the haemodynamic picture; cultures and antibiotics should be considered but IV fluid replacement takes priority.
This is correct — massive transepidermal water and protein loss causes hypovolaemia and hypoalbuminaemia, the primary driver of haemodynamic collapse in erythroderma.
High-output cardiac failure is a recognised but less immediately life-threatening complication; it does not explain the hypotension and confusion as primarily as hypovolaemia does.
Hypothermia from disrupted thermoregulation is present (temperature 35.8°C) and contributes to morbidity but is not the primary cause of hypotension in this clinical context.
Methotrexate hepatotoxicity is a chronic concern; acute hepatic failure is not the immediate driver of his haemodynamic collapse.
clinicalPearl: Erythroderma is a dermatological emergency — patients can lose litres of fluid and significant protein through inflamed skin; IV fluids and monitoring for hypothermia are immediate priorities.
keyClue: Greater than 90% BSA erythema with scaling, hypotension, tachycardia, and confusion after stopping psoriasis treatment
commonExamTrap: Focusing on sepsis as the primary cause without recognising that transepidermal fluid/protein loss is the dominant acute mechanism in erythroderma.
missedConcept: Erythroderma disrupts the skin barrier causing massive fluid and protein loss — the most immediately dangerous complication requiring urgent fluid resuscitation.
redFlagReason: Erythroderma with haemodynamic instability (hypotension, tachycardia, confusion) is a life-threatening emergency; delayed fluid resuscitation risks cardiovascular collapse and death.

clinicalStem: A 14-year-old girl is referred by her GP with a 6-month history of patchy hair loss. Examination reveals multiple well-demarcated, smooth, non-scarring patches of alopecia over the scalp with exclamation-mark hairs at the margins. Scalp skin appears normal. Her nails show fine pitting. She has had one episode of isolated patch alopecia at age 8 that resolved spontaneously. You diagnose alopecia areata. Which of the following features in this patient is most associated with a poorer prognosis for hair regrowth?
options: Age of onset in childhood
Nail pitting
Presence of exclamation-mark hairs
Previous episode with spontaneous resolution
Multiple small patches rather than a single large patch
explanation: Nail pitting and other nail changes (trachyonychia, onycholysis) in alopecia areata reflect underlying nail matrix involvement, indicating a more severe, widespread autoimmune process. Nail involvement is consistently associated with a poorer prognosis for hair regrowth and a higher risk of progression to alopecia totalis or universalis. Other poor prognostic indicators include ophiasis pattern, extensive scalp involvement (>50%), young age of onset, positive family history, and atopy. Exclamation-mark hairs are a feature of active disease, not prognosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Childhood onset does carry a slightly worse prognosis overall, but it is not the single most specific poor prognostic marker in the options given compared to nail involvement.
This is correct — nail pitting indicates nail matrix autoimmune involvement, which is a recognised marker of more severe disease and poorer hair regrowth prognosis.
Exclamation-mark hairs confirm active disease at patch margins but are a diagnostic feature, not a prognostic marker for long-term outcome.
A previous episode with spontaneous resolution is actually a relatively positive prognostic indicator, suggesting the immune process may self-limit.
Multiple small patches are less ominous than large confluent or ophiasis-pattern involvement; extensive rather than patchy disease is the prognostic concern.
clinicalPearl: Nail involvement (pitting, trachyonychia) in alopecia areata signals severe disease and predicts poorer hair regrowth — always examine the nails.
keyClue: Nail pitting in a teenager with alopecia areata
commonExamTrap: Interpreting exclamation-mark hairs as a prognostic rather than diagnostic feature of active alopecia areata.
missedConcept: Nail changes in alopecia areata indicate systemic autoimmune severity and are the strongest clinically evident poor prognostic feature among the given options.

clinicalStem: A 61-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus and peripheral vascular disease is seen in primary care with discoloured, thickened, crumbling toenails affecting all ten toes for the past four years. Nail clippings confirm Trichophyton rubrum. He completed a 12-week course of oral terbinafine 18 months ago but the infection recurred within 6 months of completing treatment. Which factor most significantly predicts failure of antifungal therapy and recurrence in this patient?
options: The causative organism (Trichophyton rubrum) being relatively terbinafine-resistant
Duration of infection exceeding 2 years before treatment initiation
Coexisting peripheral vascular disease impairing drug delivery to nails
His age making nail growth too slow for adequate antifungal clearance
Involvement of all ten toenails indicating high fungal burden
explanation: Peripheral vascular disease significantly impairs the delivery of systemically administered antifungals to the nail bed and nail matrix via the bloodstream. Since oral terbinafine depends on vascular delivery to reach therapeutic concentrations in the nail, reduced blood flow is a major independent predictor of treatment failure and relapse. Diabetes alone increases susceptibility to fungal infections, but the combination with peripheral vascular disease creates a scenario where even correct systemic therapy cannot achieve adequate nail drug levels. This is the single most important modifiable treatment challenge in this patient.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Trichophyton rubrum is the most common cause of onychomycosis and is generally sensitive to terbinafine; resistance, while possible, is uncommon and not the primary explanation for recurrence in this clinical context.
Long duration of infection is a poor prognostic factor, but it does not override the critical importance of vascular supply in determining drug delivery to the nail.
This is correct — peripheral vascular disease reduces blood flow to distal extremities, limiting the concentration of orally administered terbinafine reaching the nail, causing treatment failure.
While slower nail growth in the elderly prolongs treatment duration needed, age alone does not predict failure as powerfully as vascular supply does.
Involvement of all ten nails (total dystrophic onychomycosis) is a poor prognostic marker, but impaired vascular delivery is the mechanistically primary reason for treatment failure in this patient.
clinicalPearl: Peripheral vascular disease is the strongest predictor of oral antifungal failure in onychomycosis — the drug cannot reach the nail bed if blood supply is compromised.
keyClue: Recurrent onychomycosis despite adequate systemic terbinafine in a patient with peripheral vascular disease
commonExamTrap: Attributing recurrence to antifungal resistance rather than to vascular insufficiency limiting drug delivery in patients with peripheral arterial disease.
missedConcept: Oral antifungals require intact vascular supply to reach therapeutic nail concentrations — peripheral vascular disease is the dominant predictor of treatment failure in onychomycosis.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman of South Asian origin (Fitzpatrick skin type IV) presents with dark brown patches on her cheeks that appeared six months after a severe episode of acne. The patches correspond exactly to the sites of her previous acne lesions. Dermoscopy shows epidermal pigmentation. She is otherwise well. She asks how long the pigmentation will last. Which statement best describes the prognosis of her post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH)?
options: The pigmentation will resolve within 4 weeks with regular moisturisation
PIH in darker skin types typically takes longer to resolve and may persist for years without treatment
Dermal PIH has a better prognosis than epidermal PIH as it is more superficial
Her skin type provides natural protection, making her PIH less likely to persist than in lighter skin types
Once present for more than 6 months, PIH becomes permanent and cannot be treated
explanation: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation results from excess melanin deposited in the epidermis and/or dermis following skin inflammation. In darker Fitzpatrick skin types (IV–VI), melanocytes are more reactive and produce greater quantities of melanin in response to inflammation. As a result, PIH in darker skin types is typically more intense, covers a wider area, and takes significantly longer to resolve — often 12–24 months or more without treatment. Topical depigmenting agents (hydroquinone, kojic acid, azelaic acid) combined with broad-spectrum sunscreen are the mainstay of treatment.
wrongAnswerExplanations: PIH, particularly in darker skin types, does not resolve within 4 weeks with moisturisation; resolution is slow and typically requires targeted depigmenting therapy and sun protection.
This is correct — darker Fitzpatrick skin types have more reactive melanocytes, making PIH more intense, slower to resolve, and often requiring active treatment for months to years.
Dermal PIH is actually more difficult to treat and has a poorer prognosis than epidermal PIH; epidermal pigmentation responds better to topical agents.
Darker skin types are more prone to PIH, not less — the greater baseline melanocyte activity leads to more pronounced and persistent hyperpigmentation.
PIH even after 6 months can be treated effectively with depigmenting agents and sunscreen; it is not inherently permanent.
clinicalPearl: PIH is more severe and slower to resolve in darker Fitzpatrick skin types — combine a topical depigmenting agent with daily broad-spectrum sunscreen and manage the underlying cause.
keyClue: Dark brown patches at prior acne sites in a South Asian woman (Fitzpatrick type IV)
commonExamTrap: Assuming darker skin types are protected from PIH — in fact they are disproportionately affected due to more reactive melanocytes.
missedConcept: Fitzpatrick skin type is the key prognostic determinant in PIH; darker types experience more severe, longer-lasting pigmentation requiring active management.

topic: Cellulitis — markers of poor prognosis and follow-up
clinicalStem: A 55-year-old obese woman with type 2 diabetes presents with her fourth episode of lower leg cellulitis in 18 months. Each episode was treated successfully with oral antibiotics. On examination the left leg is erythematous, warm, and oedematous. There is also non-pitting chronic oedema of both legs from mid-calf to the ankles. She has had no skin breakdown between episodes. Which long-term complication are the recurrent episodes of cellulitis most likely to be causing?
options: Progression to necrotising fasciitis
Chronic lymphoedema from lymphatic vessel damage
Systemic amyloidosis from repeated bacteraemia
Venous insufficiency from deep venous thrombosis during each episode
Development of antibiotic-resistant skin flora
explanation: Repeated episodes of cellulitis damage cutaneous lymphatic vessels through inflammation and fibrosis. This progressively impairs lymphatic drainage, leading to chronic lymphoedema. Critically, chronic lymphoedema itself is a predisposing factor for further episodes of cellulitis, creating a vicious cycle: cellulitis damages lymphatics → lymphoedema develops → lymphoedema predisposes to further cellulitis. The non-pitting oedema already present in this patient reflects early chronic lymphatic insufficiency from her recurrent infections. Long-term management includes compression therapy, limb elevation, and prophylactic antibiotics (penicillin V) if episodes occur more than two times per year.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Progression to necrotising fasciitis is a risk with severe individual cellulitis episodes, but it is not the long-term complication caused by recurrent episodes over 18 months.
This is correct — repeated cellulitis fibroses lymphatic channels, causing progressive chronic lymphoedema that then predisposes to further cellulitis in a self-perpetuating cycle.
Systemic amyloidosis from recurrent bacterial infections (secondary amyloidosis) requires many years of chronic suppurative infection; it is not the expected complication from recurrent cellulitis.
DVT can complicate acute cellulitis, but it does not account for the bilateral non-pitting oedema pattern seen here, which is lymphatic rather than venous in origin.
Antibiotic resistance is a concern with prophylaxis but is not the specific long-term complication of the repeated infections themselves.
clinicalPearl: Recurrent cellulitis damages lymphatics causing chronic lymphoedema, which in turn promotes further cellulitis — break the cycle with compression stockings and low-dose prophylactic penicillin.
keyClue: Fourth episode of cellulitis in 18 months with bilateral non-pitting chronic oedema
commonExamTrap: Focusing only on antibiotic treatment without recognising and managing the underlying lymphoedema that is perpetuating recurrent cellulitis.
missedConcept: Recurrent cellulitis causes lymphatic damage and progressive lymphoedema, which creates a vicious cycle; prevention with compression and prophylactic antibiotics is essential.

topic: Androgenetic alopecia — complications and psychosocial impact
subtopic: Female pattern hair loss and associated endocrine disease
clinicalStem: A 26-year-old woman presents with a 12-month history of progressive hair thinning predominantly over the crown and vertex, with preservation of the frontal hairline. She reports irregular menstrual cycles and has noticed increased facial hair over her upper lip and chin over the past year. On examination there is diffuse thinning in a Christmas-tree pattern on the vertex. Her BMI is 29 kg/m². Scalp biopsy is not yet performed. Which investigation is most important to perform before attributing her hair loss to androgenetic alopecia?
options: Serum ferritin and full blood count to exclude iron deficiency
Scalp dermoscopy to assess miniaturised follicle density
Serum testosterone, DHEAS, and LH:FSH ratio
Thyroid function tests and anti-TPO antibodies
24-hour urinary free cortisol to exclude Cushing syndrome
explanation: This young woman presents with features of hyperandrogenism: frontal-sparing diffuse vertex alopecia, irregular menstrual cycles, and hirsutism in a woman of reproductive age. This clinical constellation strongly suggests polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) or another androgen-excess disorder as the underlying cause, rather than idiopathic androgenetic alopecia. Measuring serum total and free testosterone, DHEAS (to assess adrenal androgen excess), and the LH:FSH ratio is the most important next step to identify the endocrine cause. Identifying and treating the underlying hyperandrogen state is necessary for effective management.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Iron deficiency can cause diffuse telogen effluvium and should be checked, but it does not explain the hirsutism and menstrual irregularity; the androgen profile takes priority here.
Dermoscopy is a useful adjunct in alopecia diagnosis but does not identify an underlying endocrine cause; it is a structural rather than aetiological investigation.

This is correct — the combination of vertex alopecia, hirsutism, and menstrual irregularity in a young woman demands exclusion of androgen excess disorders (PCOS, adrenal cause) before labelling it as androgenetic alopecia.
Thyroid disease can cause diffuse hair loss but does not explain hirsutism and menstrual irregularity; thyroid tests are secondary to androgen profiling in this presentation.
Cushing syndrome causes hair loss but other features (central obesity, striae, hypertension, moon face) are absent; 24-hour urinary cortisol is not the priority investigation here.
clinicalPearl: In a young woman with vertex alopecia, hirsutism, and irregular periods, always measure androgens and LH:FSH before diagnosing androgenetic alopecia — PCOS must be excluded.
keyClue: Young woman with vertex alopecia, hirsutism, and irregular menstrual cycles
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing androgenetic alopecia in a young woman without investigating for underlying hyperandrogenism, missing a treatable endocrine cause.
missedConcept: Androgenetic alopecia in young women must prompt exclusion of hyperandrogenism (PCOS, CAH, androgen-secreting tumour) — hirsutism and menstrual irregularity are key red flags.

topic: Invasive fungal infection in immunocompromised host
clinicalStem: A 48-year-old man with acute myeloid leukaemia is on cycle 3 of chemotherapy. He develops a fever of 39.2°C unresponsive to 72 hours of broad-spectrum antibiotics. He now has multiple erythematous papules and nodules (0.5–2 cm) on his trunk and limbs that appeared rapidly over 24 hours. His absolute neutrophil count is 0.1 × 10⁹/L. Blood cultures taken 24 hours ago are still negative. What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most important next step in management?
options: Drug hypersensitivity reaction — stop all suspected medications immediately
Disseminated candidiasis — initiate systemic antifungal therapy empirically without waiting for culture results
Bacterial septicaemia with cutaneous emboli — add gram-positive cover to existing antibiotics
Sweet syndrome — commence high-dose systemic corticosteroids
Cutaneous lymphoma progression — arrange urgent skin biopsy before any treatment
explanation: Disseminated candidiasis (haematogenous Candida infection) classically presents in profoundly neutropenic patients with fever unresponsive to antibacterial antibiotics and characteristic erythematous papules and nodules that reflect haematogenous seeding of the skin. Blood cultures are negative in up to 50% of proven disseminated candidiasis cases. In a profoundly neutropenic patient, this is a potentially fatal infection and treatment must not be delayed for culture confirmation. Empirical systemic antifungal therapy (echinocandin first-line: anidulafungin, micafungin, or caspofungin) should be started immediately. Skin biopsy showing yeast or hyphae in dermis can confirm the diagnosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Drug hypersensitivity can cause morbilliform rashes but typically does not produce this pattern of nodular papules in a neutropenic febrile patient; stopping all medications delays antifungal treatment, which is dangerous.

This is correct — disseminated candidiasis is a life-threatening infection in neutropenic patients; empirical antifungal therapy with an echinocandin must be started immediately without waiting for cultures.

Gram-positive bacteraemia (e.g., Staphylococcal emboli) is a differential but the pattern of persistent fever unresponsive to antibiotics with new nodular skin lesions in a neutropenic patient strongly favours disseminated fungal infection.

Sweet syndrome (acute febrile neutrophilic dermatosis) can complicate AML but typically presents as tender, well-demarcated erythematous plaques; corticosteroids would be harmful if fungal infection is the cause.

Cutaneous lymphoma progression would not cause such an acute presentation over 24 hours with fever in a neutropenic patient; delaying antifungal therapy to await biopsy results risks fatal deterioration.
clinicalPearl: In a neutropenic patient, fever unresponsive to antibiotics plus new skin papules/nodules equals disseminated candidiasis until proven otherwise — start empirical echinocandin therapy immediately.
keyClue: Profound neutropenia, fever unresponsive to antibiotics, and new erythematous nodules in a leukaemia patient on chemotherapy
commonExamTrap: Waiting for blood culture confirmation before starting antifungal therapy — cultures are negative in up to 50% of disseminated candidiasis and delay is fatal.
missedConcept: Disseminated candidiasis is a dermatological emergency in neutropenic patients; skin nodules are a haematogenous seeding pattern requiring immediate empirical antifungal therapy.
redFlagReason: Disseminated candidiasis in a profoundly neutropenic patient carries very high mortality if antifungal therapy is delayed waiting for blood culture confirmation.

subtopic: Sun exposure as a relapse trigger
clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman of mixed Hispanic heritage was treated successfully for melasma over 4 months with hydroquinone 4% cream and tretinoin, achieving near-complete clearance. She returns 3 months later with recurrence of the same brown patches on her cheeks and upper lip. She reports that she stopped the creams once the pigmentation cleared and is now on a combined oral contraceptive pill. She works outdoors as a landscape gardener. She wants to know what she should do differently to prevent another relapse. Which single measure would most effectively reduce the risk of further melasma recurrence?
options: Switch from combined OCP to progesterone-only pill to reduce oestrogen-driven pigmentation
Daily application of broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen with iron oxide to all sun-exposed facial areas
Restart hydroquinone immediately and continue indefinitely without breaks
Avoid outdoor work entirely during daylight hours
Apply topical corticosteroid cream to prevent inflammatory pigment production
explanation: Melasma is notoriously prone to relapse, and UV radiation (including visible light) is the most important exogenous trigger for melanocyte stimulation and pigment recurrence. Broad-spectrum sunscreen with SPF 50+ containing iron oxide is particularly important in melasma as it blocks both UVA/UVB and visible light, which can independently stimulate melanogenesis in darker skin types. Daily sunscreen use even after clearance is the most evidence-based single intervention for preventing relapse. This woman's outdoor work makes consistent daily photoprotection critical. Combined OCPs are a risk factor but switching alone without sun protection is insufficient.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Switching to a progesterone-only pill may reduce one hormonal trigger but does not address UV exposure, which is the dominant and most modifiable recurrence trigger for this outdoor worker.
This is correct — broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen with iron oxide is the single most effective maintenance strategy to prevent UV-triggered melasma relapse.
Continuous hydroquinone use carries risks of exogenous ochronosis and irritation; it should be used in cycles, not indefinitely, and is a treatment rather than the primary prevention tool.
Avoiding outdoor work entirely is not realistic or necessary; appropriate photoprotection (sunscreen, hat, UV-protective clothing) achieves adequate protection.

Topical corticosteroids are used in triple combination products for melasma treatment but have no role as a standalone prevention strategy and risk skin atrophy and rebound hyperpigmentation with prolonged use.
clinicalPearl: Melasma always requires maintenance photoprotection with daily broad-spectrum SPF 50+ (with iron oxide to block visible light) — stopping sunscreen after clearance almost guarantees relapse.
keyClue: Recurrent melasma in an outdoor worker who stopped treatment after clearance and is not using sunscreen
commonExamTrap: Focusing only on hormonal triggers (OCP) without addressing UV exposure, which is the dominant and most modifiable recurrence factor.
missedConcept: Sunscreen is the cornerstone of melasma relapse prevention — treatment response means nothing if daily photoprotection is not maintained lifelong.

subtopic: Psoriasis as an independent cardiovascular risk factor
clinicalStem: A 44-year-old non-smoking man with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis affecting 25% of his body surface area presents for a routine dermatology follow-up. He is on methotrexate. His blood pressure is 136/84 mmHg, BMI is 31 kg/m², and fasting glucose is 5.9 mmol/L. His lipid profile and the rest of his examination are normal. He asks whether his psoriasis has any implications beyond his skin. Which of the following best describes the systemic risk associated with his psoriasis and what follow-up is required?
options: Psoriasis has no systemic implications beyond the skin; only skin-directed treatment and follow-up are needed
His main risk is infection due to methotrexate immunosuppression; annual chest X-ray is the priority
Moderate-to-severe psoriasis is an independent risk factor for cardiovascular disease, metabolic syndrome, and psoriatic arthritis, requiring proactive screening
Psoriasis confers an increased risk of gastrointestinal malignancy, and colonoscopy is the highest-priority investigation
The primary long-term complication of psoriasis is progression to erythroderma in all patients on methotrexate
explanation: Psoriasis is now recognised as a systemic inflammatory disease, not merely a skin condition. Moderate-to-severe psoriasis independently increases the risk of major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) by up to 58% compared to controls, equivalent to adding 10 years to a patient's cardiovascular age. This patient also has borderline hypertension, overweight BMI, and impaired fasting glucose — components of metabolic syndrome. Psoriatic arthritis develops in up to 30% of psoriasis patients. Follow-up should include annual cardiovascular risk assessment, blood pressure monitoring, fasting glucose, lipid profile, and musculoskeletal screening for psoriatic arthritis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Psoriasis is definitively associated with systemic comorbidities including cardiovascular disease, metabolic syndrome, and psoriatic arthritis — dismissing systemic implications is a significant clinical error.
Infection risk from methotrexate is a valid concern but is not the primary long-term systemic risk; annual chest X-ray is not the recommended standard monitoring for methotrexate in skin disease.

This is correct — moderate-to-severe psoriasis is an independent cardiovascular risk factor and increases risk of psoriatic arthritis; proactive screening for cardiovascular risk factors and joint disease is mandatory.
Psoriasis is not primarily associated with gastrointestinal malignancy; colonoscopy is not indicated as a priority screening tool in this context.
Progression to erythroderma is rare and typically triggered by inappropriate treatment withdrawal, not by methotrexate therapy itself.
clinicalPearl: Treat psoriasis as a systemic inflammatory disease — screen annually for cardiovascular risk factors and psoriatic arthritis, as these cause greater long-term morbidity than the skin disease itself.
keyClue: Moderate-to-severe psoriasis (25% BSA) in a middle-aged man with borderline metabolic syndrome features
commonExamTrap: Viewing psoriasis as a skin-only disease and neglecting cardiovascular, metabolic, and musculoskeletal screening in follow-up.
missedConcept: Psoriasis is an independent cardiovascular risk factor with systemic inflammatory implications; proactive comorbidity screening is essential and often more impactful than skin treatment alone.

topic: Lichen planopilaris — prognosis and scarring alopecia
subtopic: Distinguishing scarring from non-scarring alopecia in follow-up
clinicalStem: A 52-year-old woman presents with an 18-month history of patchy hair loss associated with scalp itching and burning. On examination there are several discrete areas of alopecia on the vertex and parietal scalp. The scalp skin within the bald patches is smooth, shiny, and pale. There is perifollicular erythema and scale at the active border of the patches. Hair pull test is positive at the advancing margins. Dermoscopy shows absent follicular openings in the centre of lesions. Which statement about the prognosis of this condition is most accurate?
options: Hair will regrow fully once systemic steroids are commenced, as with alopecia areata
The hair loss is reversible if treatment is started within 12 months of onset
The follicles in the central bald patches have been permanently destroyed and hair will not regrow in those areas
This condition is self-limiting and hair usually regrows spontaneously within 2 years
The absent follicular openings on dermoscopy indicate a fungal cause requiring systemic antifungal therapy
explanation: This presentation is classic for lichen planopilaris (LPP), a primary lymphocytic scarring alopecia. The smooth, pale, follicle-absent scalp skin within established patches, absent follicular ostia on dermoscopy, and perifollicular inflammation at the active border are hallmarks of scarring alopecia with follicular destruction. Once follicles are destroyed in LPP, the damage is permanent — hair will never regrow in those areas. Treatment aims to halt disease activity at the advancing margin and prevent further scarring, not to restore lost hair. This is a critical point for patient counselling and distinguishes LPP from non-scarring alopecias.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Unlike alopecia areata (non-scarring), LPP permanently destroys follicles — systemic steroids may suppress active inflammation but cannot regenerate follicles already lost in scarred areas.

Reversibility depends on whether follicles have been permanently destroyed; once scarring is established (smooth, follicle-absent scalp), that hair loss is irreversible regardless of treatment timing.

This is correct — absent follicular openings indicate follicular destruction and permanent scarring; hair cannot regrow in areas where follicles have been obliterated by the lichenoid inflammatory process.
LPP is not self-limiting; without treatment it progresses to involve larger areas of scalp with permanent hair loss — spontaneous resolution is not expected.

Absent follicular openings in dermoscopy are characteristic of scarring alopecia (follicular destruction), not fungal infection; fungal infections cause non-scarring alopecia with preserved follicles.
clinicalPearl: Absent follicular ostia on dermoscopy = irreversible scarring — counsel patients that established bald patches from LPP are permanent; treatment only halts progression.
keyClue: Smooth shiny scalp with absent follicular openings centrally and active perifollicular inflammation at the border
commonExamTrap: Treating lichen planopilaris like alopecia areata and promising hair regrowth — scarring alopecias permanently destroy follicles and this expectation leads to patient harm.
missedConcept: Distinguishing scarring from non-scarring alopecia is essential — the absence of follicular openings on dermoscopy indicates irreversible follicular destruction; management goals are disease arrest, not regrowth.
